3A_Bibliographic.citation: Keyes, K. M., Hamilton, A., Finsaas, M., & Kreski, N. (2024). Childhood internalizing, externalizing and attention symptoms predict changes in social and nonsocial screen time. Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology, 59, 2279-2290.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Among these youth, 9,066 had complete information on psychiatric symptoms, screen time, and demographics (described below), suggesting 9.3% of the initial follow-up sample had missingness. This suggests that missingness was not substantial and was left unimputed. ABCD data release 4.0 was used, and a propensity weight was used for all analyses approximating American Community Survey sociodemographic of 9 and 10-year-olds.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Psychiatric symptoms (CBCL total problems, internalizing, externalizing, attention, depressive, anxious-depressive, withdrawn depressive, aggressive, rule-breaking subscales) as trichotomized variables predicting screen time
3C_Exposure.modalities: social screen time (social media, texting, video chatting), nonsocial screen time (TV/movies, YouTube, video games)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Trichotomized based on T-score cut-points: non-significant symptoms (reference), borderline symptoms, clinical symptoms. Screen time also trichotomized by percentile rank into low (reference), medium, and high.
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for screen time; Parent-report for psychiatric symptoms
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: The Youth Screen Time Survey queried youth about the amount of time they spent using screens for six different activities at baseline and follow up one year later, based on a previously validated measure. Items were rated on a 7-point scale (0=none; 0.25=<30 min; 0.5=30 min; 1=1 h; 2=2 h; 3=3 h; 4=4+ hours). Activities were queried separately for typical weekend versus weekday use. We constructed social screen time variables, which summed time spent texting, using social media, and video chatting separately on weekends and weekdays, and nonsocial screen time variables, which summed time spent watching TV shows or movies, watching videos on YouTube, and playing video games, also separately on weekend and weekday.
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper tests BIDIRECTIONAL associations. The PRIMARY analysis is psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time (reverse causation direction). Supplementary analyses examine screen time predicting psychiatric symptoms.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Screen time use one year later (social and nonsocial, weekday and weekend) - primary analysis. Supplementary: psychiatric symptoms one year later.
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Youth Screen Time Survey (for primary); CBCL (for supplementary)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: We modeled the effects of psychiatric symptoms, with each scale and subscale measured as trichotomized variables (ref=Non-significant symptoms), on screen use, measured as trichotomous variables due to skewness, using cumulative logistic regression in R using the VGAM package. All models controlled for screen use at baseline, thus the models are residualized change models.
3D_Outcome.notes: Primary outcome is screen time; this paper primarily examines whether psych symptoms predict subsequent screen time (reverse causation). The outcome domain is coded as other since screen time is the outcome.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Cumulative logistic regression (ordinal) using VGAM package in R; residualized change models controlling for baseline screen use
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: baseline screen use, sex, age, race and ethnicity, household income
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Our primary analysis examined psychiatric symptoms as independent variables and weekday and weekend and social and nonsocial screen time use one year later as dependent variables. We modeled the effects of psychiatric symptoms, with each scale and subscale measured as trichotomized variables (ref=Non-significant symptoms), on screen use, measured as trichotomous variables due to skewness, using cumulative logistic regression in R using the VGAM package. All models controlled for screen use at baseline, thus the models are residualized change models.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Cumulative OR for higher screen time category
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Considering total psychiatric problems, clinical levels of problems predicted higher levels of weekday social (OR=1.02, 95% CI 1.01-1.02)

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: magnitudes of association were generally small across all comparisons... the magnitude of any effects were generally of small magnitude... it is worth noting that these associations, while consistent, are smaller
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper consistently uses CIs and explicitly acknowledges small effect sizes. No p-values reported. Abstract uses ORs and CIs but does not use significance language.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Ensuring that ongoing data collection and analysis efforts rigorously attend to temporality and transitions, as well as consideration of alternative causes, will accelerate progress in the field.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations are primarily research-oriented rather than clinical/policy.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion is notably cautious and balanced. It emphasizes that associations are bidirectional and of comparable magnitude in both directions, explicitly noting small effect sizes throughout. The paper frames findings as supporting selection effects (children with symptoms select into different media use patterns) rather than screen time causing harm. The authors caution against reflexively concluding correlations reflect a causal path from screen time to mental health.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: The primary direction (psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time) is unusual for this literature. Effect sizes are reported primarily in Supplementary Tables. Many ORs are very close to 1.0 with narrow CIs, consistent with the large sample.
